summary: Gruber recounts his 30-minute guided demo of Apple Vision Pro at Apple Park, describing the technology as extraordinary and exceeding his already-high expectations. He details the passthrough video quality, the eye-and-hand navigation model, the spatial stability of virtual elements, and three categories of experience: productivity computing, 2D/3D content consumption, and fully immersive environments. While he declines to predict whether Vision Pro will succeed as a product, he expresses confidence that VisionOS will become Apple's next major platform alongside macOS and iOS. He frames spatial computing as a fundamental removal of the display boundary abstraction, comparing its significance to the iPhone's introduction of multitouch.
key_points: The passthrough video is so natural and latency-free that navigating a real room while wearing the headset requires no extra concentration — it feels like looking through safety glasses, not at screens inside a headset.

VisionOS's navigation model — look at a target with your eyes, tap finger and thumb — is a breakthrough in simplicity that becomes natural within minutes, with eye-tracking-based spatial stability that makes virtual elements feel as real as physical objects.

The elimination of display boundaries is the conceptual leap: your physical room becomes the canvas for arranging app windows of any size, removing the rectangular constraint that defines every other computing platform.
Fully immersive sports demos shot with custom camera rigs were so compelling that Gruber says he'd consider buying Vision Pro if sporting events were its only feature.
Despite the technological achievement, Gruber is careful not to predict commercial success — the $3,500 price, the weight on the face, and the unfinished software all remain open questions.
money_quotes: Vision Pro and VisionOS feel like they've been pulled forward in time from the future.
I haven't had that feeling about a new product since the original iPhone in 2007.
It does not magically look indistinguishable from real life, but it does not feel like looking at a screen at all.
It is simply uncanny how Vision Pro makes virtual elements as spatially stable as the walls and furniture and people in the room around you.
The constraints of even the largest physical display simply do not exist with VisionOS.
In the same way that the introduction of multitouch with the iPhone removed a layer of conceptual abstraction, VisionOS removes a layer of abstraction spatially.
It seems as profoundly different from watching regular TV telecasts of sports as TV telecasts are from audio-only radio broadcasts.
I would genuinely consider buying a Vision Pro if the one and only thing it did was show entire sporting events like this.
key_insight: Vision Pro's breakthrough is not any single technology but the elimination of the display as a boundary — spatial computing makes your physical world the canvas, removing the rectangular abstraction that has defined every computing platform since the Macintosh.
